text: The objective of this video is to illustrate the feasibility of a minimally invasive approach for a radical nephrectomy with a vena cava trombectomy and a retroperitoneal lymph node dissection. The case is a seventy-nine-year-old male with general symptoms and hematuria diagnosed with a large renal mass with a renal vein thrombus reaching the ostium and pathological lymph nodes seen on a CT scan. Clinical data from this patient were collected after patient consent into the French kidney cancer database. The patient was installed on his right side tilted at seventy degrees and the trocars inserted a bit more towards the midline than usual to allow access to the aorta and the vena cava.

The surgeon accesses the retroperitoneal space through the colic mesentery, revealing the aorta and vena cava. The left renal vein is visible with a thrombus floating inside. The dissection continues to control the renal artery, which is clipped and divided.

The surgeon performs a dissection of the cable and inter outer cable lymph nodes on the anterior wall of the vena cava and aorta. After completing the lymph node dissection, the left colon is lowered to expose and clip the ureter, which is then divided. The dissection follows the ureter towards the kidney, reaching the renal pedicle. The fourth robotic arm lifts the kidney to extend the renal vein, facilitating lateral clamping of the vena cava under the thrombus. The tension on the kidney is adjusted to ensure secure clamping.

The surgeon carefully pushes the thrombus back into the renal vein to position a vascular clip, preventing tumor spillage into the peritoneal cavity. The renal vein is divided with safe margins on the vena cava, followed by a running suture of 4-0 Prolene to close the vena cava opening. The clamp is removed, and the water tightness of the suture is tested.

The surgeon performs an adrenalectomy and additional lymph node dissection during the upper portion of the nephrectomy. The surgical field reveals the kidney and surrounding structures, with the pathology report indicating poorly differentiated renal collecting duct carcinoma and negative surgical margins.

The patient was discharged on past operative day four without any past operative complication. The patient suffered from metastatic progression at past operative month three, which was treated by anti-angiogenic therapy.
